hero.headline: From the First Dial‑Up to the Age of 5G

welcome.title: Welcome to Your Digital Journey
paragraphs: Pakistan's internet story is one of resilience, growth, and transformation. From the first public dial-up connections in the 90s to today's blazing fast 5G trials, it's been a revolutionary ride.
This website offers a deep look at how internet connectivity has reshaped Pakistan’s education, economy, culture, and digital identity.
We highlight historic milestones, analyze current usage trends, and look ahead to future possibilities in connectivity and technology.

highlights.title: The First Connections
highlights.detail: Early ISPs such as Digicom (1995), BrainNET (1996), and Paknet (PTCL) offered dial-up, mainly to universities, institutions, and early adopters. Internet cafes began appearing in major cities like Karachi and Lahore.

highlights.title: Broadband Revolution
highlights.detail: Micronet Broadband introduced DSL in 2001, followed by PTCL’s nationwide DSL project in 2007. Wateen launched WiMAX in 2007, and Wi-Tribe followed in 2009 with prepaid wireless internet.

highlights.title: 3G, 4G & Beyond
highlights.detail: Pakistan’s first 3G and 4G licenses were auctioned in April 2014. By May, Ufone, Mobilink, Telenor, and Zong had launched 3G, while Zong also rolled out 4G.

highlights.title: Rise of the Digital Economy
highlights.detail: With more users online, Pakistan became a top global freelancer hub. E-commerce platforms, mobile wallets, EdTech, and YouTubers have all flourished, reshaping how Pakistanis work, learn, and earn.

facts.title: Did You Know?
list: Pakistan had over 130 million broadband subscriptions by 2024, with ~111–116 million unique internet users.
The country ranks among the top 5 nations for freelance earnings.
3G/4G coverage grew from ~60% of population in 2018 to ~81% in 2024, driven by USF rural projects.
YouTube is Pakistan’s most visited website after Google.
StormFiber offers up to 1 Gbps residential fiber; Nayatel and others provide high-speed FTTH (300 Mbps+), with gigabit available in select plans.

cta.headline: Why This Project Matters
points: The internet isn’t just technology. It's an equalizer. It connects people to education, healthcare, and economic opportunities.
In Pakistan, the web is helping students attend virtual classes, freelancers earn global income, and communities access vital services.
This website documents the history, celebrates the present, and imagines the future of Pakistan’s digital growth.
